title_en: The Attribute of Speech (Kalam) and the Quran
title_ar: صفة الكلام وما يتعلق بالقرآن الكريم
content_en:
The attribute of divine speech (kalam) and the related question of the nature of the Quran constitute one of the most historically significant and theologically complex discussions in Islamic theology. The controversy surrounding the createdness or uncreatedness of the Quran was at the center of a major theological crisis in the third century of Islam — the Mihna — and its resolution shaped the contours of Sunni theology for all subsequent centuries. Al-Bayhaqi's treatment of this attribute reflects the mature Ash'ari position that emerged from and responded to that controversy.

The Ash'ari position on divine speech, of which al-Bayhaqi is a major exponent, distinguishes between two aspects of the Quran. The first is the eternal, divine speech (al-kalam an-nafsi) — the attribute subsisting in the divine essence, uncreated, eternal, and inseparable from Allah's being. This eternal speech is not composed of letters, sounds, or language in the way human speech is; it is a divine reality beyond human categories. The second is the expression of this eternal speech in human language, through letters, words, and sounds — what is recited, written in copies of the Quran, and memorized by human beings. This expressed form is created in the sense that the language, the sounds, and the physical copies involve created realities, but it conveys the uncreated eternal divine speech.

The Hanbali position, associated with Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal and his followers, holds that the Quran in all its aspects — as recited, written, and memorized — is the uncreated word of Allah, and that to describe any aspect of it as 'created' is a dangerous deviation from orthodoxy. This position arose in the context of the Mu'tazilite doctrine of the createdness of the Quran (khalq al-Quran), which the Hanbalis saw as an attack on the divine nature of revelation. Al-Bayhaqi's Ash'ari formulation seeks to preserve the essence of the Hanbali concern — that the content and meaning of the Quran are uncreated — while introducing a distinction that the Mu'tazilite position had conflated.

Al-Bayhaqi cites extensive hadith evidence affirming that Allah speaks — that divine speech is a real attribute, not merely a metaphor. He quotes the verse: 'And Allah spoke directly to Moses' (4:164), and discusses the night of the Prophet's ascent (Mi'raj) when he received divine communication directly. He also discusses the hadith of the divine speech on the Day of Judgment, when Allah will address the believers without any intermediary. These texts affirm that divine speech is real, though its manner transcends human categories.

The relationship between the Quran as divine speech and the Quran as a text that human beings read, memorize, and handle is carefully analyzed. Al-Bayhaqi affirms that the meaning and content of the Quran — its guidance, its laws, its narratives, its descriptions of Allah — all derive from the eternal uncreated divine speech. The Quran is therefore authoritative, sacred, and normative in an absolute sense that no human text can be. Yet the physical copies, the ink, the paper, and the human acts of recitation are created realities, as are all physical things. This nuanced position seeks to honor both the absolute sacredness of the Quran and the theological requirement that creation be distinguished from the Creator.

Al-Bayhaqi's treatment of divine speech also encompasses the divine names and their relationship to the attribute of kalam. The divine names are part of Allah's speech — they are how Allah has described Himself in revelation. The obligation to learn, remember, and call upon Allah by His names (du'a) is therefore an engagement with divine speech itself. The Quran instructs: 'And to Allah belong the best names, so invoke Him by them' (7:180). The devotional practice of calling upon Allah by His names is grounded in the recognition that these names are not human inventions but revealed designations through which Allah has made Himself known to His servants.
content_ar: كلام الله تعالى صفة قائمة بذاته أزلية أبدية، والقرآن كلام الله غير مخلوق.
